Home / Tools & Workshop / Tools & Workshop Hub

Tools and Garage: The Complete Homeowner Guide

The hub for cordless tools, battery care, charging safety and workshop setup - what to own, how to keep it running, and how to fix it when it stops.

Tools & Workshop

By Louis F.

Electric Vehicle & Home Efficiency Specialist

July 9, 2026 6 min read

On this page

  1. When something won’t work
  2. Batteries: the part that actually needs you
  3. Keeping tools alive
  4. Buying in: platforms before tools
  5. The space itself
  6. Quick answers
  7. The bottom line

Somewhere in your garage right now there’s probably a drill with a dead battery, a charger with a blinking light you’ve never decoded, and a mower you’re hoping starts next spring. That’s normal. Battery tools have quietly taken over the homeowner workshop — they’re better than the gas and corded stuff they replaced in almost every way — but they brought a new set of skills with them, and nobody handed out the manual. How you charge, store and winter a lithium-ion pack now matters more than how you oil a hinge ever did.

This hub is that manual. It’s organized the way problems actually show up: something won’t work, something needs charging or storing, you’re buying into a platform, or you’re setting up the space itself. Each section points to a full guide.

When something won’t work

Start here, because this is why most people land on a page like this. Nine times out of ten the “broken” thing isn’t broken.

If a tool runs rough, stops under load, or won’t start at all, work through the cordless tool troubleshooting guide — it separates the two-minute fixes (contacts, battery seating, overheat lockouts) from the genuine failures, and it’s honest about which repairs a homeowner should not attempt.

If a battery won’t charge, the charger’s blinking light is trying to tell you something specific. Most “dead” packs are actually in a temperature lockout or a protective deep-sleep, not dead. The battery won’t charge troubleshooting guide decodes the light patterns brand by brand, and the safe battery revival guide covers what you can legitimately do about a pack the charger refuses — and why the jump-start trick all over YouTube is the one method to skip.

Batteries: the part that actually needs you

Here’s the mental shift battery tools require: the tool is durable, the battery is perishable, and almost everything that shortens a battery’s life happens while you’re not using it. Left on the charger for weeks. Stored dead. Stored full. Frozen in the garage all winter. Baked on a sunny windowsill in July.

What kills lithium-ion tool batteries (roughly in order)
Stored dead (deep discharge)
Worst
Heat - hot garage, sun, hot charging
Severe
Living on the charger at 100%
High
Charging below freezing
High
Normal use and honest wear
Low

The counter-moves are simple and they’re spelled out in three guides. How to charge cordless tool batteries safely covers the rules that matter: room-temperature charging (roughly 40–104°F), original chargers only, off the charger when done. How to store cordless power tools safely makes the case for the battery-first approach — packs out of the tools, at 40–50% charge, somewhere that doesn’t freeze or bake. And the battery tool safety guide covers the part nobody wants to think about: what thermal runaway is, how to spot a failing pack (swelling, heat, odor), and where damaged batteries actually go — hint: never the trash, and not the retail drop-off bin either.

If you’re ready to give charging a proper home instead of a random outlet, the charging station setup guide covers the wall, and the fire-resistant battery cabinet guide covers containment for households with a lot of packs.

Keeping tools alive

Maintenance on cordless tools is refreshingly small — no oil, no spark plugs, no carburetors — but it’s not zero, and the few tasks that exist are the difference between a drill that lasts fifteen years and one that cooks its motor in three. The cordless tool maintenance guide covers the whole loop: vents, contacts, chucks, and the safe wipe-down methods that don’t push liquid into a motor. The air compressor guide handles the one shop machine that can rust itself to death from the inside if you never drain the tank.

Outdoor power gear gets its own pair: how to maintain battery lawn tools for the in-season care, and how to store a battery mower, trimmer and blower for winter for the October ritual that decides whether spring starts with mowing or with troubleshooting.

Buying in: platforms before tools

The single most consequential decision in cordless tools isn’t which drill to buy — it’s which battery platform to marry. Batteries and chargers only work within one brand’s system, they’re where the real money goes, and switching later means abandoning that investment. Before any individual tool purchase, read how to choose the right battery platform; if you’re weighing the big shop brands head-to-head, the DeWalt vs Milwaukee vs Ryobi vs Makita ecosystem comparison lays out what each system is genuinely good at without pretending there’s one right answer.

Then, and only then, decide what to actually buy. Cordless power tools homeowners actually need is the anti-shopping-list: the handful of tools that earn their place, the ones that can wait, and the ones most people regret. On the yard side, battery mower vs gas mower settles the biggest single question with real trade-offs instead of cheerleading.

The space itself

A workshop that fights you gets abandoned, and most homeowner workshops fail on the same three things: not enough light where the cutting happens, no dedicated home for charging so batteries end up on whatever surface was empty, and a floor plan that puts the person between the tool and the door. How to use power tools safely in a small garage covers the layout, lighting and workflow habits that make a one-car garage work like a shop — where the cutting happens, where the batteries live, what needs light, and the handful of habits that prevent the classic small-space accidents.

Quick answers

What charge level should batteries be stored at? Around 40–50%, off the charger, somewhere that stays above freezing. Full and empty are both bad places for a lithium-ion pack to sit.

Why won’t my battery charge after winter in the garage? Usually cold lockout (warm it indoors a couple of hours) or deep discharge from sitting — the charger reads a deeply drained pack as defective. The won’t-charge guide walks the whole diagnosis.

Are battery tools worth it over gas and corded? For nearly all homeowner use, yes — instant start, no fuel, little maintenance. The honest exceptions are all-day heavy work and a few big-acreage yard cases, covered in the mower comparison.

Do I really have to stay on one battery brand? Within a system, yes — packs aren’t cross-compatible, and adapter workarounds void warranties. That’s why the platform decision comes before the first tool.

Where do old tool batteries go? Free drop-off bins at major hardware retailers handle healthy packs (tape the terminals). Swollen or damaged packs are different — those go through household hazardous waste. Details in the safety guide.

The bottom line

Battery tools rewrote the homeowner workshop, and the skills that matter now are battery skills: charge at room temperature, store at half-charge, keep packs out of the heat and cold, decode the blinking light before declaring anything dead, and treat a swollen pack as the hazard it is. Every guide linked above goes deep on one piece of that. Pick the one that matches today’s problem — there’s always one.

Educational content, not professional advice. Follow your tool and battery manuals, and take damaged batteries to proper disposal. Curious what your chargers and shop gear add to the bill? Run the numbers in the Sim-Lab.

Written by Louis F.

Electric Vehicle & Home Efficiency Specialist

A developer and digital creator dedicated to simplifying the intersection of electric vehicle ownership and home energy management.
